title: UCB — Immunology — briefing Q3-2025
html:
Bimzelx Quarterly Briefing -- Q3 2025 (July - September 2025)

Quarter Summary

Q3 2025 was a pivotal quarter for Bimzelx, marked by the completion and delivery of ZoomRx's first wave of HCP-Patient Conversation research, continued commercial momentum across expanding indications, and important competitive developments in the IL-17 vs. IL-23 landscape. UCB reported strong financial performance, with Bimzelx establishing itself as the company's primary revenue growth engine.

1. ZoomRx Deliverables This Quarter

HCP-Patient Conversations Final Report (July 2025)

The flagship deliverable this quarter was the HCP-Pt Final Report, covering 30 recorded conversations from 26 HCPs (Dermatologists and NP/PAs) collected between May 20 and June 19, 2025. This was preceded by:

  • Interim Report (June 2025): 19 conversations from 19 HCPs, providing early directional findings
  • Interim Report Ver2 (June 17, 2025): Updated with additional conversations and treatment sequence analysis
  • Final Report (July 3, 2025): Complete analysis with 30 conversations
  • Final Report v6 (July 23, 2025): Refined version for distribution to internal UCB teams beyond the core study sponsors

Key clients at UCB receiving these deliverables include JC Punzalan and the broader immunology commercial team.

Key Findings Delivered to UCB

  1. Efficacy drives prescribing but not conversation: Efficacy is the #1 Rx driver but receives ~10% of discussion time; safety and dosing dominate conversation
  2. 3-layer decision funnel: Treatment history assessment, then patient-driven RoA choice, then HCP-driven single-product selection (23/30 single product)
  3. Safety perception barriers: GI history is the only safety concern that demonstrably changes Bimzelx Rx; suicide risk warning creates effort burden but does not change clinical decisions
  4. Segment-level variation: Bimzelx Believers (2/7 prescribed), Dabblers (3/9 considered then prescribed), Convert IL-17A Writers (most cited lack of familiarity)
  5. Behavioral science overlay: Effort aversion, familiarity bias, and framing effects systematically disadvantage newer entrants

September 4, 2025 Report Update

A September 4 version of the Final Report was produced incorporating:

  • Consolidated behavioral science findings as a standalone section
  • Refined segment-level analysis with commentary for each HCP segment
  • Enhanced treatment pathway analysis with quote support
  • Appendix with additional conversation findings
  • Tag system for distinguishing conversation-based insights vs. screener/chart data vs. behavioral science insights

2. Commercial Performance

Bimzelx Revenue Trajectory

While detailed Q3-specific revenue was reported as part of UCB's H1 2025 results (reported earlier in the year), the trajectory through this period showed continued strong uptake:

  • Bimzelx was on track for the EUR 2.23B full-year 2025 result, representing >200% growth from EUR 607M in 2024
  • PsA indication (approved September 2024) was ramping through its first full year
  • HS indication (approved November 2024) was in early commercial launch phase
  • US launch dynamics were favorable: by early 2025, half of surveyed rheumatologists had already trialed Bimzelx in PsA

Market Access Developments

  • Insurance access remained a cited barrier in ZoomRx conversations, with one HCP noting: "The main reason for not selecting Bimzelx was only because it's harder to get, and we don't have any samples yet"
  • Prior authorization requirements for Bimzelx are more burdensome than for established competitors (Cosentyx, Taltz)
  • UCB's patient assistance programs were being discussed by reps but had limited visibility in actual HCP-patient conversations

UCB Client Financial Signals — Q3 2025

UCB reported ~EUR 1.9B total revenue for Q3 2025 (+22% YoY), with Bimzelx delivering ~EUR 500M quarterly (+250% YoY) — establishing itself as the primary growth engine. Rystiggo + Zilbrysq contributed ~EUR 80M combined.

What UCB management said on the Q3 2025 call:

  1. "Bimzelx is on track to be a EUR 4B+ peak sales franchise" — management framed this as a platform story, not a single-indication drug, emphasizing they are "building across 5 indications."
  2. US launch momentum "exceeded internal expectations" in PsO, with market share gains highlighted. HS approval (December 2024) was beginning to add incremental revenue.
  3. Management addressed the IL-23 competition directly: "We compete on speed and depth of response; IL-17A/F dual inhibition is mechanistically differentiated" — positioning against Skyrizi on clinical grounds rather than class identity.
  4. UCB described 2025 as a "breakout year" — the tone was very bullish, with Bimzelx driving the corporate narrative.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. Analysts pressed on AbbVie/Skyrizi competition — UCB's response focused on mechanistic differentiation rather than conceding the IL-23 narrative, suggesting confidence in the efficacy-depth argument.
  2. Analysts raised the GI candidiasis safety signal — management characterized it as "manageable; real-world rates consistent with clinical trials," indicating the signal has not escalated beyond the known profile.
  3. The tone of analyst questions suggested growing interest in Bimzelx's multi-indication trajectory, validating UCB's platform positioning.

3. Competitor Earnings Intelligence — Q3 2025

Revenue Snapshot — Q3 2025

Company Brand Q3 2025 Revenue YoY Growth Key Signal
AbbVie Skyrizi ~$3.8B +55% IL-23 class anchor; UC approved, CD label expansion ongoing
AbbVie Rinvoq ~$1.0B +50% JAK gaining in IBD despite lingering safety perceptions
AbbVie Humira ~$2.0B Declining Biosimilar erosion; promotional spend shifting to Skyrizi/Rinvoq
J&J TREMFYA ~$1.0B +35% PsO growth; pursuing UC/CD. "Convenient IL-23 with one-press injector"
Novartis Cosentyx ~$1.3B +5% Growth slowing; biosimilar entry expected 2026-2027; IV formulation launched
Eli Lilly Taltz ~$700M Flat Deprioritized — Lilly focused on GLP-1/obesity (Mounjaro/Zepbound)
Amgen Otezla ~$570M -5% Declining as biologics gain share in moderate-severe psoriasis
Bimzelx ~EUR 500M est. ~+200% Fastest-growing injectable biologic in derm; HS launch fueling acceleration

AbbVie — Skyrizi ($3.8B) + Rinvoq ($1.0B) + Humira ($2.0B)

From AbbVie Q3 2025 earnings commentary:

  1. Skyrizi ~$3.8B, +55% — "the anchor of our growth": AbbVie management explicitly named Skyrizi as the franchise cornerstone. UC approval and CD label expansion are broadening the Skyrizi prescriber base — GI specialists now prescribe Skyrizi alongside dermatologists, creating a flywheel that Bimzelx (with no GI indications) cannot match.

  2. Skyrizi is dominating IL-23 class narrative: AbbVie frames Skyrizi as the default IL-23, pushing the narrative that IL-23 is the preferred biologic class. This directly challenges Bimzelx's IL-17 positioning — if dermatologists accept "IL-23 first," Bimzelx must win on superior efficacy depth rather than class preference.

  3. Humira ~$2.0B, declining — promotional shift complete: As Humira erodes, AbbVie has fully redirected its massive derm sales force toward Skyrizi and Rinvoq. Bimzelx faces the full intensity of AbbVie's commercial machine.

Implication for UCB: AbbVie's combined immunology franchise (~$6.8B in Q3 alone) dwarfs Bimzelx. UCB's competitive strategy cannot be volume-based — it must be precision-targeted: PsA patients where IL-17A/F dual inhibition offers demonstrably faster, deeper response than IL-23, and HS where Skyrizi has no footprint.

Johnson & Johnson — TREMFYA (~$1.0B, +35%)

From J&J Q3 2025 earnings commentary:

  1. TREMFYA gaining in PsO and pursuing UC/CD: J&J is positioning TREMFYA as the "convenient IL-23 with one-press injector" — a dosing/administration play rather than an efficacy differentiation play.

  2. GI expansion is the strategic bet: TREMFYA's pursuit of UC/CD gives it the same cross-indication momentum as Skyrizi. If approved, two IL-23s will have GI labels, further entrenching the class in treatment algorithms.

Implication for UCB: TREMFYA's growth in PsO is modest enough that Bimzelx's faster growth rate should maintain competitive position. The real threat is TREMFYA's GI expansion creating cross-specialty prescribing habits that default to IL-23 in derm as well.

Novartis — Cosentyx (~$1.3B, +5%)

From Novartis Q3 2025 earnings commentary:

  1. Growth slowing to single digits: Cosentyx at ~$1.3B quarterly is approaching maturity. Biosimilar entry expected 2026-2027 will accelerate the decline.

  2. IV formulation launched as lifecycle extension: Novartis launched IV Cosentyx to differentiate in PsA/AxSpA with faster onset and loading dose advantage.

Implication for UCB: Cosentyx's slowdown is a tailwind for Bimzelx. Patients and payers looking beyond first-gen IL-17 are natural Bimzelx candidates. Critically, biosimilar Cosentyx could HELP Bimzelx — by increasing IL-17 class familiarity and accessibility, it positions Bimzelx as the "next-gen IL-17" upgrade.

Eli Lilly — Taltz (~$700M, flat)

From Lilly Q3 2025 context:

  1. Taltz deprioritized: Lilly's entire commercial apparatus is focused on Mounjaro/Zepbound. Derm sales force attention is directed elsewhere. Taltz is essentially self-maintaining.

Implication for UCB: Taltz attrition is an immediate opportunity pool. Dermatologists switching off Taltz choose between Skyrizi, TREMFYA, and Bimzelx — and Bimzelx's dual IL-17A/F mechanism is the most natural "stay in IL-17 class, but upgrade" option.

Amgen — Otezla (~$570M, -5%)

From Amgen Q3 2025 context:

  1. Otezla declining as biologics gain share: The oral PDE4 inhibitor is losing ground to IL-17/IL-23 biologics in moderate-severe psoriasis.

Implication for UCB: Otezla's decline signals that the "oral convenience" argument alone cannot sustain share against superior biologic efficacy. This validates Bimzelx's injectable-but-superior-efficacy positioning — patients are willing to inject for better outcomes.

Synthesis: What Competitor Earnings Tell UCB

  1. AbbVie's immunology machine is ~$6.8B per quarter and growing — Bimzelx's growth rate is higher, but the absolute revenue gap is enormous. UCB wins by targeting specific patient segments (PsA, HS, skin-only severe psoriasis) rather than competing on breadth.

  2. The IL-23 narrative is hardening — both AbbVie and J&J are positioning IL-23 as the preferred biologic class. Bimzelx's counter-narrative must be evidence-based superiority in specific domains (speed of response, depth of clearance in PsA).

  3. Cosentyx biosimilar is a double-edged sword — it could cannibalize IL-17 class share OR increase class familiarity. UCB should prepare positioning for both scenarios.

  4. Taltz and Otezla attrition = acquisition opportunity — ~$1.3B of quarterly competitor revenue is in managed decline. These patients need a new home.

4. Competitive Landscape Developments

IL-23 Dominance Continues

  • Skyrizi continued its dominant run in PsO, on track for $17.3B in 2025 global sales. AbbVie's Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis approvals for Skyrizi broadened its dermatologist touchpoints
  • Tremfya grew 40% year-over-year to $5.2B in 2025 sales, benefiting from PsA approval and pipeline expansion
  • Real-world studies published in 2025 (British Association of Dermatologists Biologics and Immunomodulators Register) confirmed IL-23p19 inhibitors offer the longest drug survival, reinforcing the durability advantage

IL-17 Class Dynamics

  • Cosentyx biosimilar competition looming on the horizon, potentially reshaping IL-17 class economics
  • Taltz maintained its position as the most commonly prescribed IL-17 among HCPs unfamiliar with Bimzelx (per ZoomRx data, Taltz was the default for "Convert IL-17A Writers")
  • Bimzelx continued to differentiate on dual IL-17A/F mechanism and faster onset of action

Emerging Safety Landscape

  • Early real-world data beginning to accumulate on differential safety profiles between IL-17 and IL-23 classes
  • IL-23 inhibitors associated with reduced mortality and infection risks but increased NMSC (non-melanoma skin cancer) risks relative to IL-17 agents in emerging observational data
  • These nuances not yet widely incorporated into clinical practice but may influence class-level positioning over time

5. What ZoomRx Should Know

Account Management Insights

  • The HCP-Pt Conversation study was well-received by UCB. The behavioral science overlay added significant perceived value beyond standard conversation analysis
  • UCB is categorized with three HCP segments (Believers, Dabblers, Convert IL-17A Writers) that align directly with commercial targeting strategy -- ZoomRx's research validated and enriched this segmentation
  • The study prompt was updated mid-fielding to focus specifically on IL-17 and IL-23 conversations (vs. all advanced systemics), indicating UCB's primary competitive focus is the IL-17 vs. IL-23 dynamic
  • UCB's study team includes Richard Durante (Principal oversight), Sneha Shah (Senior Manager), Pranav UT and Vipasha Purohit (Engagement Managers)

Next Steps Flagged

  • Strategy workshop proposed to make research findings actionable (noted in Final Report)
  • Qualitative deep dive proposed to understand why HCPs take specific approaches in patient conversations
  • Wave 2 conversation collection likely, given the study design supports continuous collection with customizable prompts
  • Integration opportunity with PET (Promotional Effectiveness Tracking) -- the original proposal positioned HCP-Pt Conversations as complementary to PET, and this integration remains relevant

6. Implications for ZoomRx Portfolio

Cross-Client Relevance

  • The behavioral science framework developed for UCB (effort aversion, familiarity bias, framing effect, patient catastrophic thinking) is applicable across ZoomRx's portfolio -- could be templated for other HCP-Pt conversation studies
  • The "single product presentation" finding (23/30 HCPs) has implications for any client in a competitive biologic market -- this dynamic likely exists across oncology, rheumatology, and other specialty areas

Revenue Opportunity

  • UCB's Bimzelx momentum (EUR 2.23B in sales, 5 indications) positions UCB as an expanding account
  • HS-specific research represents a greenfield opportunity -- different prescriber audience, different competitive set
  • PET integration and longitudinal tracking offer recurring revenue potential
